perturbation.original_text:
10. Limited Warranty
10.1 Tripath provides an end user limited warranty for retail Products, and one-year limited warranty for OEM products. The terms of the end user limited warranty are set forth in documentation provided with the retail Product.
10.2 For OEM Products, Tripath warrants that the Products will be free of manufacturing and workmanship defects for one year from the date of shipment by Distributor to a customer, or eighteen months from the date of shipment to Distributor, whichever is sooner.
10.3 If any OEM Products prove defective during this one year period as set forth in Section 10.2, Tripath will, at its option, either (a) repair or replace the Product with a new or reconditioned Product, (b) use reasonable efforts to provide Distributor with a correction of the defect, or (c) refund to Distributor the purchase price paid for the Product. Any Product replaced will become Tripath property.
10.4 Distributor must return the defective OEM Product pursuant to a return material authorization under Section 9.1 in the original shipping carton (or an equivalent protective carton), and pay the shipping and insurance charges. Distributor assumes the risk of loss or damage during shipment.
10.5 OEM Products that are defective as set forth in Section 10.2 as a result of improper handling, incorrect installation or other misuse, or as a result of accident, disaster, acts of God or other such events, as determined by Tripath in its reasonable discretion, are not covered by this limited warranty.
10.6 For OEM Products, Tripath provides only the warranties set forth in this limited one year limited warranty. For retail Products, Tripath provides to the end user only the warranties set forth in the applicable end user warranty. Any and all other warranties, either express, implied or statutory, including, without limitation, warranties of fitness for a particular purpose, or of merchantability, are hereby expressly excluded and disclaimed by Tripath.
perturbation.changed_text:
10. Limited Warranty
10.1 Tripath may provide a limited warranty for Products at its discretion. The terms of any warranty will be determined solely by Tripath.
10.2 Tripath has no obligation to provide any warranty for OEM Products.
10.3 If any Products are deemed defective, Tripath may, at its option, offer assistance, but is not obligated to do so.
10.4 Distributor must handle any returns of defective Products according to Tripath's policies, which may be changed at any time.
10.5 Tripath's determination of defects is final.
10.6 Tripath provides no other warranties, express, implied or statutory.
perturbation.explanation: The original text provides a limited warranty, while the modified text removes the warranty and replaces it with discretionary language, diminishing consumer rights and violating implied warranty laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Warranty of Merchantability under California Commercial Code
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification eliminates any implied warranty of merchantability, which California law provides for the sale of goods. By stating that Tripath has no obligation to provide any warranty and disclaiming all other warranties, the contract violates the implied warranty provisions of the California Commercial Code.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, COM 2314. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Commercial Code - COM DIVISION 2. SALES [2101 - 2801] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 3. General Obligation and Construction of Contract [2301 - 2328] ( Chapter 3 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 2314. (1) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2316), a warranty that the goods shall be merchantable is implied in a contract for their sale if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind. Under this section the serving for value of food or drink to be consumed either on the premises or elsewhere is a sale. (2) Goods to be merchantable must be at least such as (a) Pass without objection in the trade under the contract description; and (b) In the case of fungible goods, are of fair average quality within the description; and (c) Are fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used; and (d) Run, within the variations permitted by the agreement, of even kind, quality and quantity within each unit and among all units involved; and (e) Are adequately contained, packaged, and labeled as the agreement may require; and (f) Conform to the promises or affirmations of fact made on the container or label if any. (3) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2316) other implied warranties may arise from course of dealing or usage of trade. (Enacted
perturbation.justification: The modification explicitly removes the original warranty provisions and replaces them with discretionary language that diminishes consumer rights. Specifically, the original text provided a one-year limited warranty for OEM products, while the changed text states that Tripath has no obligation to provide any warranty for OEM Products and disclaims all other warranties. This contradicts the implied warranty of merchantability as outlined in California Commercial Code 2314, which states that "Unless excluded or modified (Section 2316), a warranty that the goods shall be merchantable is implied in a contract for their sale if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind."

perturbation.original_text:
9. Returns
9.1 Distributor may return Products only after receiving written authorization from Tripath. All authorized returns will be shipped by Distributor freight prepaid. Unauthorized returns may be reshipped to Distributor, freight collect.
-3- Distributor Agreement 9.2 Distributor may request stock rotation return of slow moving Products, which will be authorized by Tripath provided that: (a) the Product is contained in Tripath's then current Distributor Price List, (b) the Product is packaged as it was originally shipped, and (c) a purchase order for equal value accompanies the request for return. Such stock rotation returns will not exceed five percent (5%) of the dollar value of Distributor's previous six (6) months net purchase orders from Tripath.
9.3 Discontinued Product may be returned only with prior written authorization.
9.4 Credit will be allowed for returned Products based on the price actually paid by Distributor, less any prior DPAs, credits and other allowances.
9.5 If, in the reasonable opinion of Tripath, previously authorized returned Products have been used or damaged, a credit or replacement may be refused. Distributor will be notified within one month after receipt of Product by Tripath of any such Product Tripath is claiming to be used or damaged.
perturbation.changed_text:
9. Returns
9.1 Distributor may return Products only at Tripath's sole discretion. Returns will be handled according to Tripath's policies, which may change without notice. Tripath is not responsible for shipping costs for any returns.
9.2 Stock rotation returns are not permitted.
9.3 Discontinued Products are not returnable.
9.4 Credit for returned Products will be determined solely by Tripath.
9.5 Tripath's decision on the condition of returned Products is final.
perturbation.explanation: The original text allows for returns under specific conditions, while the modified text eliminates any guaranteed return process, violating consumer protection regulations related to defective or non-conforming goods.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification severely restricts the distributor's rights to return defective products, effectively removing the protections provided by the Song-Beverly Consumer Warranty Act which mandates that manufacturers provide avenues for repair, replacement, or refund of goods under warranty. By making returns entirely discretionary and denying stock rotation, the agreement contradicts the spirit and requirements of the act.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1.7. CONSUMER WARRANTIES [1790 - 1797.96] ( Title 1.7 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1333. ) CHAPTER 1. Consumer Warranty Protection [1790 - 1795.8] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1333. ) ARTICLE 3. Sale Warranties [1792 - 1795.8] ( Article 3 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1333. ) 1793.2. (a) Every manufacturer of consumer goods sold in this state and for which the manufacturer has made an express warranty shall: (1) (A) Maintain in this state sufficient service and repair facilities reasonably close to all areas where its consumer goods are sold to carry out the terms of those warranties or designate and authorize in this state as service and repair facilities independent repair or service facilities reasonably close to all areas where its consumer goods are sold to carry out the terms of the warranties. (B) As a means of complying with this paragraph, a manufacturer may enter into warranty service contracts with independent service and repair facilities. The warranty service contracts may provide for a fixed schedule of rates to be charged
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the distributor's rights to return defective products and receive credit, making returns entirely discretionary. This contradicts the spirit of consumer warranty protections, as described in the Law Explanation, and directly opposes the manufacturer's obligation to provide service and repair facilities as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1: 'Every manufacturer of consumer goods sold in this state and for which the manufacturer has made an express warranty shall...Maintain in this state sufficient service and repair facilities reasonably close to all areas where its consumer goods are sold to carry out the terms of those warranties...'

perturbation.original_text:
20. Limitation of Liability
20.1 Neither Tripath nor Distributor will be liable for any incidental or consequential -7- Distributor Agreement damages (including, without limitation, damages for loss of business profits, business interruption, loss of business information, or other pecuniary loss) arising out of this Agreement, even if advised of the possibility of such damages.
perturbation.changed_text:
20. Limitation of Liability
20.1 Neither Tripath nor Distributor will be liable for any damages arising out of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original text limits liability for incidental or consequential damages. The modified text removes the clauses in parenthesis, suggesting a further limitation of liability than that previously established in the contract, which could be misconstrued and lead to consumer harm.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Civil Code Section 1668 - Contracts Contrary to Public Policy
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 1668 states that 'All contracts which have for their object, directly or indirectly, to exempt anyone from responsibility for his own fraud, or willful injury to the person or property of another, or violation of law, whether willful or negligent, are against the policy of the law.' The modified text removes the examples of incidental and consequential damages, and could be interpreted as an attempt to limit all liability for fraud, willful injury, or violation of law, which is against public policy, as stated in the Civil Code.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 4. UNLAWFUL CONTRACTS [1667 - 1670.12] ( Title 4 enacted 1872. ) 1668. All contracts which have for their object, directly or indirectly, to exempt any one from responsibility for his own fraud, or willful injury to the person or property of another, or violation of law, whether willful or negligent, are against the policy of the law. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes specific examples of damages (loss of business profits, business interruption, etc.) previously excluded from liability. The original text limited liability for incidental or consequential damages. The modified text expands the limitation of liability by removing these clauses. California Civil Code Section 1668 states that contracts cannot exempt anyone from responsibility for their own fraud, willful injury, or violation of law. While the original text limited liability for specific types of damages, the modified text's broader limitation could be interpreted as attempting to limit liability even for fraud or willful injury, which would contradict Section 1668, as explained in the Law Explanation. The 'Omission Legal Contradiction' definition applies because the changed text removes legally relevant information creating a loophole. This undermines consumer protection, making the contract non-compliant. The removal expands liability limitations, creating a possible conflict with the law.
